narrative:
The market remains in a business as usual regime, with a stress score of 22 out of 100 confirming a calm underlying environment. What holds it there is the combination of contained credit spreads, orderly volatility, and broad sector stability, even as equities have pulled back modestly over the past five sessions.

VIX at 18.9 sits at the 52nd percentile of its ten-year history, meaning volatility is roughly in the middle of its historical range rather than elevated, and the contango term structure signals no near-term hedging urgency from the options market. High yield spreads at 276 basis points are in the 13th percentile of their five-year range, and investment grade at 77 basis points carries a z-score of negative 1.02, placing both firmly in historically tight territory. Credit is not just confirming the absence of equity stress, it is actively contradicting any bearish read on the week's price action.

EEM and EWZ stand out as the sharpest movers over the five-day window, falling 6.2 percent and 5.6 percent respectively, pointing to a specific bout of emerging market weakness rather than broad global risk-off. XLK dropped 5.9 percent over the same period, suggesting technology was a primary drag on the SPY return, while the rotation into XLV, XLRE, and XLE hints at a defensive and income-oriented reallocation within US equities.
